revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[de:Imashirozuka Kofun]][[en:Imashirozuka Kofun]][[fr:Kofun d'Imashirozuka]][[ja:今城塚古墳]]
{{Short description|Kofun period Kofun in Takatsuki, Japan}} {{Infobox ancient site|type = {{ill|Zenpokoenfun|en|Zenpokoenfun|qid=Q11268718|lt=Zenpokoenfun|1=Zenpōkōenfun}} | name =Imashirozuka Kofun | native_name =今城塚古墳 | native_name_lang = | image = File:Imashirozuka Kofun, zenkei.jpg | image_size = | alt = | caption = Imashirozuka Kofun | map_type = Japan Osaka Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = location in Japan | relief = 1 | map_caption = Imashirozuka Kofun | map_size = 270px | altitude_m = | altitude_ref = | coordinates = {{Coord|34.850254|N|135.594158|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | map_dot_label = | location = {{ill|Takatsuki|qid=Q335423|1=Takatsuki}} | region = [[:en:Kinai region|Kinai region]] | type = Kofun | part_of ={{ill|Mishima Kofun Cluster|qid=Q17211948|1=Mishima Kofun Cluster}} | length = | width = | area = | volume = | diameter = | circumference = | height = | built = 6th century | abandoned = | epochs =[[Kofun period]] | cultures = | discovered = | excavations =1997-2007 | archaeologists = | condition = | ownership = | management = | public_access = Yes (on site museum) | Historic Site = | website = | architectural_styles ={{ill|keyhole-shaped kofun|qid=Q11268718|1=Zenpōkōenfun|2=ja|3=前方後円墳|4=ko|5=전방후원분|6=zh|7=前方後圓墳}} | architectural_details = |notes = {{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=brown|text align=center|[[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]]}}{{maplink2|type=point|frame=yes|plain=yes|zoom=14|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|coord={{coord|34|51|0.92|N|135|35|38.99|E}}}} | commemorates= {{ill|Keitai|qid=Q349277|1=Emperor Keitai}} }}
{{nihongo|'''Imashirozuka Kofun'''|今城塚古墳|}} is a [[Kofun period]] {{ill|Zenpokoenfun|en|Zenpokoenfun|qid=Q11268718|lt=keyhole-shaped Kofun|1=Zenpōkōenfun}}, located in the Gungeshinmachi neighborhood of the city of [[:en:Takatsuki, Osaka|Takatsuki, Osaka]] in the [[:en:Kinai region|Kinai region]] of {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|qid=Q17|lt=Japan|1=Japan}}. The tumulus was designated a [[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]] in 1958, with the area under protection expanded in 2006.<ref>{{cite web |url= https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/138814|title=今城塚古墳 附新池埴輪製作遺跡|language=ja |publisher=Agency for Cultural Affairs |accessdate=August 22, 2022}}</ref> It is the largest ''kofun'' in the {{ill|Yodo River|en|Yodo River|qid=Q1140505|lt=Yodo River|1=Yodo River}} basin and one of the largest constructed in the first half of the sixth century. Although it has not been sanctioned by the {{ill|Imperial Household Agency|en|Imperial Household Agency|qid=Q1143602|lt=Imperial Household Agency|1=Imperial Household Agency}}, there is a credible theory that it is the true tomb of the 26th {{ill|Emperor Keitai|en|Emperor Keitai|qid=Q349277|lt=Emperor Keitai|1=Emperor Keitai}}.
==Overview== Imashirozuka Kofun is located near the center of the Mishima Plain, orientated to the west. It is 350 meters long and 340 meters wide and was surrounded by a double moat. The central tumulus is accompanied by several ''baicho'' auxiliary tumuli and is 190 meters long. Historical characteristics such as the mound's shape and ''{{ill|Haniwa|en|Haniwa|qid=Q849376|lt=Haniwa|1=Haniwa}}'' found on site, as well as evidence found in the ''[[Kojiki]], [[Nihon Shoki]],'' ''[[Engishiki]]'', and other historical documents suggest that it is the tomb of a "great king" of the sixth-century Yamato regime. This time corresponds to that of the semi-legendary Emperor Keitai. A committee of historians and archaeologists recommended that it be designated as Emperor Keitai's burial place; the Imperial Household Agency, however, demurred as it had already designated the [[Ōta Chausuyama Kofun]] in {{ill|Ibaraki, Osaka|en|Ibaraki, Osaka|qid=Q502403|lt=Ibaraki, Osaka|1=Ibaraki, Osaka}}, some 1.3 kilometers to the west, as that emperor's mausoleum. The decision conflicts with a view holding that the Ōta Chausuyama Kofun, having been built in the mid fifth century, predates Emperor Keitai's reign.<ref name="Shiseki">{{cite book |last1=Isomura |first1=Yukio |last2=Sakai |first2=Hideya |title=(国指定史跡事典) National Historic Site Encyclopedia |date=2012 |publisher=学生社 |isbn=4311750404}}{{in lang|ja}}</ref>
The Imashirozuka Kofun was widely thought to have been ravaged in the {{ill|Sengoku period|en|Sengoku period|qid=Q204023|lt=Sengoku period|1=Sengoku period}} when {{ill|Oda Nobunaga|en|Oda Nobunaga|qid=Q171411|lt=Oda Nobunaga|1=Oda Nobunaga}} built a fortress on the site for attacking the {{ill|Miyoshi clan|en|Miyoshi clan|qid=Q2975329|lt=Miyoshi clan|1=Miyoshi clan}} in his 1568 invasion of {{ill|Settsu Province|en|Settsu Province|qid=Q864163|lt=Settsu Province|1=Settsu Province}}; archeological evidence indicates that the damage was caused by a landslide-like collapse of the mound triggered by the {{ill|1596 Keichō–Fushimi earthquake|en|1596 Keichō–Fushimi earthquake|qid=Q11495186|lt=1596 Keichō–Fushimi earthquake|1=1596 Keichō–Fushimi earthquake}}.<ref>{{Cite web |title=高槻市ホームページはこちら |url=https://www.city.takatsuki.osaka.jp/site/history/4569.html |access-date=2024-03-03 |website=高槻市ホームページ |language=ja}}</ref>
The city of Takatsuki City has been conducting [[archaeological excavation]]s since 1997 to obtain information for developing the site into an [[:en:Archaeological park|Archaeological park]]. A large quantity of ''haniwa'' have been recovered, including 170-cm tall house-shaped one—the largest discovered in Japan, elaborate warrior ''haniwa'', and ''haniwa'' depicting falconers, {{ill|Sumo|en|Sumo|qid=Q40561|lt=wrestlers|1=Sumo}}, [[miko|shrine maiden]]s, seated boys, horses, chickens, and other animals. These ''haniwa'' were mostly from the inner levy dividing the two moats. Some of the artifacts are on display at the nearby Imashirozuka Ancient History Museum.<ref name="Shiseki"/>
The Araike haniwa production site is located about 1.5 kilometers west of Imashirozuka Kofun. It is one of the oldest and largest ''haniwa'' workshops known and was in operation for about a century from the mid-fifth to the mid-sixth century. Excavations have confirmed three large ''haniwa'' workshops, 18 kilns, and a village for craftspeople. The site was a11ed to the National Historic Site designation in 2006. It is a 25-minute walk from {{ill|Settsu-Tonda Station|en|Settsu-Tonda Station|qid=Q59513|lt=Settsu-Tonda Station|1=Settsu-Tonda Station}} on the {{ill|West Japan Railway Company|en|West Japan Railway Company|qid=Q502125|lt=West Japan Railway Company|1=West Japan Railway Company}} {{ill|JR Kyōto Line|en|JR Kyōto Line|qid=Q1195820|lt=Kyōto Line|1=JR Kyōto Line}} or {{ill|Tomita Station|en|Tomita Station|qid=Q7818979|lt=Tomita Station|1=Tomita Station}} on the {{ill|Hankyu|en|Hankyu|qid=Q473058|lt=Hankyu|1=Hankyu}} [[:en:Kyoto Main Line|Kyoto Main Line]].<ref name="Shiseki"/>
==See also== *[[:en:List of Historic Sites of Japan (Ōsaka)|List of Historic Sites of Japan (Ōsaka)]]
==References== {{reflist}}
==External links== {{template-inline|Imashirozuka-kofun}} *[https://www.city.takatsuki.osaka.jp/site/history/4569.html Takatsuko City History Web City]{{in lang|ja}}
